event: The Dilmun civilization emerges on the islands, becoming a major trading hub linking Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley.
Dilmun is referenced in Sumerian cuneiform texts as a sacred, prosperous land and key center of the copper and pearl trade.
Thousands of burial mounds are constructed across Bahrain, forming one of the largest prehistoric cemeteries in the world.
Dilmun reaches its commercial peak, controlling trade routes through the Persian Gulf from its capital near Qal'at al-Bahrain.
Bahrain falls under the influence of the Assyrian Empire as Dilmun's independent power wanes.
The islands are incorporated into the Neo-Babylonian Empire's sphere of control.
Bahrain comes under the Achaemenid Persian Empire following Cyrus the Great's conquests.
Greek explorers under Alexander the Great's admirals reach the islands, naming the main island Tylos.
Tylos becomes part of the Hellenistic world under Seleucid and Characene influence, prospering through pearl trade.
Bahrain (Tylos) falls under the control of the Parthian Empire as it dominates the Gulf trade routes.
The Sasanian Persian Empire annexes Bahrain, administering it as part of its eastern Arabian province.
Nestorian Christian communities and bishoprics are established on the islands, reflecting Bahrain's diverse religious life.
Bahrain converts to Islam during the lifetime of the Prophet Muhammad, among the earliest regions to embrace the new faith.
The Qarmatians, a radical Ismaili sect, seize Bahrain and establish a utopian republic centered at al-Hasa.
Qarmatian raiders sack Mecca and carry off the Black Stone of the Kaaba, holding it for around two decades.
An uprising led by Abu al-Bahlul al-Awwam overthrows Qarmatian rule in the region.
The Uyunid dynasty takes power in Bahrain with Abbasid Seljuk backing, ending Qarmatian dominance.
The Usfurid dynasty rises to control eastern Arabia and Bahrain after the decline of the Uyunids.
Bahrain comes under the tributary influence of the kings of Hormuz dominating Gulf commerce.
Portugal conquers Bahrain, building forts to control the lucrative pearl trade in the Persian Gulf.
A local revolt expels the Portuguese, and Bahrain passes under the rule of Safavid Persia.
Omani forces invade Bahrain, briefly wresting control from a weakening Safavid administration.
The Al Khalifa family, led by Ahmed bin Muhammad, conquers Bahrain from Persian rule, founding the dynasty that still reigns.
Bahrain signs the General Maritime Treaty with Britain, beginning the islands' alignment with British power against piracy.
Bahrain becomes a British protectorate under a treaty granting Britain control of its foreign affairs and defense.
Britain installs Isa bin Ali Al Khalifa as ruler after intervening in a succession dispute, beginning his long reign.
An exclusive agreement deepens the British protectorate, barring Bahrain from foreign relations without British consent.
Britain forces the abdication of Sheikh Isa bin Ali and installs his son Hamad, launching administrative reforms.
Oil is discovered at Jebel Dukhan, making Bahrain the first Arabian Gulf state to find petroleum and transforming its economy.
Britain establishes its main Persian Gulf naval base in Bahrain, cementing the islands' strategic importance.
Reform movements demanding political representation and labor rights emerge among Bahraini workers and merchants.
Iran formally claims Bahrain as its fourteenth province, intensifying a long-running sovereignty dispute.
The March Intifada erupts as oil workers and students strike against British control and unemployment.
A UN survey finds Bahrainis overwhelmingly favor independence, leading Iran to renounce its claim to the islands.
Bahrain declares full independence from Britain on August 15 and joins the United Nations and the Arab League.
Bahrain adopts its first constitution and holds elections for a National Assembly under Emir Isa bin Salman.
The Emir dissolves the National Assembly and suspends parts of the constitution, beginning decades of unelected rule.
Bahrain becomes a founding member of the Gulf Cooperation Council.
The King Fahd Causeway opens, connecting Bahrain to Saudi Arabia by a 25-kilometer road link.
A popular uprising demanding the restoration of the constitution and parliament begins, met with a harsh crackdown.
Sheikh Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa succeeds his late father and begins introducing political liberalization reforms.
Bahrainis overwhelmingly approve the National Action Charter in a referendum, paving the way for constitutional monarchy.
Bahrain becomes a kingdom as Hamad declares himself king, promulgates a new constitution, and restores parliamentary elections.
Bahrain hosts the first Formula One Grand Prix in the Middle East at the Bahrain International Circuit.
A US-Bahrain free trade agreement enters into force, strengthening economic ties with the United States.
Arab Spring protests centered on Pearl Roundabout are suppressed with help from a Saudi-led GCC military intervention.
Bahrain dissolves the main opposition group Wefaq and Waad as authorities tighten control after the uprising.
Bahrain signs the Abraham Accords, normalizing diplomatic relations with Israel.
